text: The surgeon makes a 5mm subumbilical incision using a #15 scalpel blade. A fan-shaped infiltration of the subcutaneous fat is performed with a mixture of saline, lidocaine, and adrenaline using a 50ml syringe connected to a 20-gauge cannula.

Ten minutes after infiltration, liposuction is performed using a dedicated syringe connected to a circulation procurement system. The peri-umbilical incision is closed, and the lipoaspirate is transferred into syringes, kept horizontally to separate excess fluids from the fat tissue.
The surgeon processes mesenchymal cells using an adipose tissue device, preparing for deployment in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a fistuloscope to visually confirm the anatomy of the fistula tract, identifying any residual abscesses, side tracts, and the internal opening. The fistuloscope also facilitates curettage and cleaning of the fistula tract.

Following infiltration with adrenalized saline, the surgeon creates an advancement flap to cover the internal opening of the fistula. The flap, composed of mucosa, submucosa, and muscular fibers, is secured at the distal margin with absorbable sutures.
The surgeon infiltrates the lipoaspirate around the external opening and along the entire fistulous tract, using a syringe to distribute the adipose tissue evenly within the surgical site.
